coredata.dc:title: Preparation, characterization and the supercapacitive behaviors of electrochemically reduced graphene quantum dots/polypyrrole hybrids
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Xinmin
Yin, Zheng-Zhi
Xu, Jiale
Li, Shan
Wang, Chengchao
Wang, Binghu
Qin, Yong
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Developing advanced materials for energy conversion and storage devices is of significant importance for modern society. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were electrostatically self-assembled with polypyrrole (PPy), and then electrochemically reduced GQDs (ERGQDs)/PPy hybrids were obtained via direct electrochemical reduction of the GQDs. The size and distribution of the PPy particles are closely correlated with the concentration of the pyrrole (Py) monomer. The obtained ERGQDs/PPy hybrids combine the advantages of electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) from ERGQDs and pseudo-capacitors from PPy, exhibiting high specific capacitance (418 F g–1 at 1 A g–1), good rate capability (75.8% capacitance retention at 10 A g–1) and high cyclic stability (86.0% capacitance retention after 1000 cycles at 10 A g–1). These results indicate that the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy hybrids might be a promising electrode material for supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical reduction
Graphene quantum dots
Polypyrrole
Hybrids
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM and TEM images of different nano-sized PPy
Cyclic voltammograms and GCD curves of PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3
TEM images of ERGQDs and ERGQDs/PPy-2
FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns of different samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LY18B050005 ), Natural Science Foundation for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province ( 20KJA150005 ), Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis ( SKLBA2108 ), Future Research Project of Jiangsu Province ( BE2017088 ) and Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center ( ACGM2016-06-27 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of ERGQDs/PPy-2.
SEM (A − C) and TEM images (D − F) of PPy-1 (A, D), PPy-2 (B, E) and PPy-3 (C, F).
Cyclic voltammograms (A) and GCD curves (B) of PPy-1/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and PPy-3/GCE in 1.0 M H2SO4. Scan rate of (A), 5 mV s − 1; current density of (B), 1 A g − 1.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE in 0.2 M PBS of pH 7.0 for 50 cycles. (B) The first cycle on the cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE in 0.2 M PBS of pH 7.0. Differential pulse voltammograms of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE in 0.2 M PBS of pH 7.0 before (C) and after (D) its electrochemical reduction.
TEM and HRTEM images of ERGQDs (A) and ERGQDs/PPy-2 (B, C).
(A) FT-IR spectra of GQDs, ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2. (B) XRD patterns of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the scan rate of 5 mV s − 1. (B) Cyclic voltammograms of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the scan rate of 5, 10, 30, 50 and 100 mV s − 1. (C) Specific capacitances of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at various scan rates.

(A) GCD curves of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the current density of 1 A g − 1. (B) GCD curves of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the current density of 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 A g − 1. (C) Specific capacitances of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 at various current densities.

Nyquist plots of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE (a), ERGQDs/GCE (b) and PPy-2/GCE (c). Inset is the corresponding equivalent circuit, where Rs represents the ohmic resistance of electrolyte and the internal resistance of electrode, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance, Wd represents the Warburg resistance, and Q represents the constant phase element.
(A) Cyclic stability of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE for 1000 cycles. (B) Specific capacitance of ERGQDs/PPy-2 under the condition of continuously altering the current density.
Important characteristic FT-IR spectra data (cm−1) of different samples.
Performance comparison between ERGQDs/PPy-2 and other systems based on carbon-PPy at 1 A g–1.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MWCNTs: multi-walled carbon nanotubes; MGO: modified graphite oxide; rGO: reduced graphene oxide; N-PCM: nitrogen-doped porous carbon matrix.

head.ce:title: Preparation, characterization and the supercapacitive behaviors of electrochemically reduced graphene quantum dots/polypyrrole hybrids

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dShandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing advanced materials for energy conversion and storage devices is of significant importance for modern society. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were electrostatically self-assembled with polypyrrole (PPy), and then electrochemically reduced GQDs (ERGQDs)/PPy hybrids were obtained via direct electrochemical reduction of the GQDs. The size and distribution of the PPy particles are closely correlated with the concentration of the pyrrole (Py) monomer. The obtained ERGQDs/PPy hybrids combine the advantages of electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) from ERGQDs and pseudo-capacitors from PPy, exhibiting high specific capacitance (418 F g–1 at 1 A g–1), good rate capability (75.8% capacitance retention at 10 A g–1) and high cyclic stability (86.0% capacitance retention after 1000 cycles at 10 A g–1). These results indicate that the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy hybrids might be a promising electrode material for supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excessive depletion of fossil fuels and the consequent environment pollution, the development of high efficient energy conversion and storage devices is of significant importance for modern industry and ecological environment [1–4]. Among the developed electric energy storage devices, lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors have attracted worldwide attention due to their dominant energy densities [5,6]. Compared with lithium-ion batteries, supercapacitors usually display higher power density, faster power delivery or uptake, longer cycling life, and better environmental compatibility [7–9]. Generally, supercapacitors can be divided into electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) and pseudo-capacitors according to the different energy storage mechanisms. Carbon-based materials are commonly used in EDLC due to their high surface area, while conducting polymers (e.g. polyaniline) and transition metal oxides (e.g. MnO2) are the promising candidates used in pseudo-capacitors [10–12]. Obviously, the hybrids of carbon-based materials and conducting polymers may combine the advantages of EDLC and pseudo-capacitors, displaying excellent supercapacitive performances [13–16].

As a polymer with intrinsic electrical conductivity, polypyrrole (PPy) has gained increasing attention in the past decades [17]. More importantly, the attractive properties of PPy such as easy synthesis, good chemical stability, and high energy storage capacity make it a potential candidate as supercapacitor electrodes [18]. However, conducting polymers are susceptible to fatigue during the repeated charging/discharging cycles, resulting in low rate capability and cycling stability [19]. Although the introduction of carbon materials to conducting polymers could address this issue through the restraint of the shrinkage or swelling of polymeric chains [20], the supercapacitive behaviors are partially limited by the insufficient hybridization of most carbon materials and the nanostructured conducting polymers. Hence, the pursuing of carbon materials with appropriate size for the hybridization with nanostructured conducting polymers seems to be a meaningful work.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel zero-dimensional graphitic carbon material with diameters from 3 to 20 nm, have received enormous attention since the combination of small size and excellent performance of graphene is achieved within GQDs [21,22]. More importantly, GQDs can be regarded as an ideal candidate to be hybridized to nanostructured conducting polymers due to their matched nano-size. However, the electrical conductivity of GQDs is seriously limited by the oxygen-containing groups, and therefore the reduction of GQDs is necessary to eliminate the oxygen-containing groups and enhance the electrochemical activity of GQDs [23]. Usually, GQDs can be reduced by hydrothermal method [24] or solvothermal method [25], however, both the two methods are tedious and time-consuming. Electrochemical reduction is simple and convenient, which might be an alternative way for the reduction of GQDs. In fact, electrochemical reduction of graphene oxide (GO) to graphene has been reported in previous literatures [26–28], suggesting the feasibility of synthesis of electrochemically reduced GQDs (ERGQDs). In addition, ERGQDs and electrochemically reduced GO are especially suitable for electrochemical applications [29].

In this work, PPy was first synthesized by chemical oxidative polymerization of pyrrole (Py), which was then electrostatically self-assembled with GQDs. After the electrochemical reduction of GQDs to ERGQDs, the resultant ERGQDs/PPy hybrids are used as the electrode material in supercapacitors as they can combine the advantages of EDLC from ERGQDs and pseudo-capacitors from PPy. On one hand, both the reduction of GQDs/PPy to ERGQDs/PPy and the assessment of supercapacitive performance of the ERGQDs/PPy are achieved by electrochemistry directly without tedious operations; on the other hand, the nanosized zero-dimensional ERGQDs match well with the nanostructured PPy in size, resulting in higher specific capacitance of the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy than other systems based on carbon-PPy previously reported. The results indicate that the obtained ERGQDs/PPy hybrids can display high specific capacitance, good rate capability and high cyclic stability. In addition, the supercapacitive behaviors of the developed ERGQDs/PPy are closely associated with the size of the PPy particles in the hybrids, which depends on the concentration of the Py monomer used.

Pyrrole (Py), citric acid monohydrate and ammonium persulfate (APS) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and Py was distilled under reduced pressure before use. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ•cm, Millipore) was used throughout.

All electrochemical experiments including cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), galvanostatic charging/discharging (GCD) testing and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., China). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterizations were conducted with a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) and a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of ERGQDs/PPy, ERGQDs, GQDs and PPy were recorded on a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of different samples were conducted on a D/max 2500 PC diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos, Japan). The electrical conductivity of different samples was measured on a model SZT-2A four-probe instrument purchased from Tongchuang Electronics Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China).

Different volumes of Py monomer (0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 mL) were dissolved in 10 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4, respectively, and the resultant Py solutions were denoted as Py-1, Py-2 and Py-3. Next, three samples of APS (0.34, 0.68 and 1.02 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of water, which were then added dropwise into the solutions of Py-1, Py-2 and Py-3, respectively. After the polymerization of Py was conducted for 5 h with continuous stirring, the products were centrifugally separated, washed, and freeze-dried at −54 °C for 24 h. The obtained PPy samples were denoted as PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3, respectively, according to the volume of Py used for polymerization.

GQDs were prepared by a bottom-up method previously reported [30,31]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid monohydrate was placed into a ceramic crucible, which was then heated at 200 °C in a muffle furnace for 30 min. After that, the products were dissolved in 10 mL of water by sonification, resulting a transparent yellow solution of GQDs (~2 mg mL−1).

Next, 4 mg of PPy-2 was dispersed into 2 mL of the above GQDs solution by sonification. Usually, conducting polymers synthesized by chemical or electrochemical oxidative polymerization are positively charged, while GQDs are negatively charged due to their abundant oxygen-containing groups of high electronegativity. The negatively charged GQDs could be self-assembled with the positively charged PPy-2 through electrostatic attraction. After 24 h of self-assembly, the obtained products (GQDs/PPy-2) was centrifugally collected, washed, and freeze-dried at −54 °C for 24 h.

4 mg of the GQDs/PPy-2 was dispersed into 2 mL of water by sonification, and then 10 μL of the aqueous dispersion (2 mg mL−1) was dropped onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm diameter) and dried under an infrared lamp. Next, ERGQDs/PPy-2 modified GCE (ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE) was prepared via direct electrochemical reduction of the GQDs/PPy-2/GCE by CV for 50 cycles (potential range: −1.4 ~ 0 V; scan rate: 100 mV s − 1) in 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.0. The electrochemical reduction was conducted in a three-electrode cell with a platinum foil as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, respectively. The preparation of ERGQDs/PPy-2 is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ERGQDs modified GCE (ERGQDs/GCE) was prepared by dropping 10 μL of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs (2 mg mL−1) onto the surface of a GCE, followed by the electrochemical reduction described above. For control experiments, other modified electrodes including PPy-1/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and PPy-3/GCE were also prepared by dropping 10 μL of the aqueous dispersions of PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3 (2 mg mL−1) onto the surface of a GCE and dried under an infrared lamp.

The electrochemical experiments were conducted in a traditional three-electrode cell by using GCE modified with different active samples as the working electrode, a platinum foil as the counter electrode and a SCE as the reference electrode, and the electrolyte was 1.0 M H2SO4. The potential range for CV and GCD was between −0.1 and 0.7 V. EIS analysis of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE, ERGQDs/GCE and PPy-2/GCE was conducted in the frequency range of 106 ~ 0.01 Hz with an alternating sinusoidal signal of 5 mV, and the cyclic stability testing of these samples was conducted at the current density of 10 A g − 1. According to Eq. (1) [32] and Eq. (2) [33], the specific capacitance (C, F g − 1) can be calculated from the cyclic voltammograms and the GCD curves, respectively, of different samples.
(1)
C = ∫
I d V
m v V
where I = current, V = potential, m = mass of sample, and v = scan rate.
(2)
C = ∫
I × t
m × V
where I = current, t = discharge time, m = mass of sample, and V = potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM images of different nano-sized PPy
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3, which were prepared from Py of different concentrations, are shown in Fig. 2 A−C. PPy-1 exhibits as typical nanoparticles of ~100 nm size with a slight aggregation (Fig. 2A). Compared with PPy-1, the size of PPy-2 is increased to ~150 nm (Fig. 2B), which can be attributed to the increased concentration of Py monomer used for the synthesis of PPy. Although the size of PPy-3 is further increased, a serious aggregation is observed for the PPy-3 nanoparticles (Fig. 2C). The poor dispersibility of PPy-3 might be ascribed to the higher concentration of Py monomer, since the polymerization rate of Py increases with increasing concentration of Py [34]. The changes in the size and the dispersibility of PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3 can also be reflected by the TEM images of these PPy samples (Fig. 2D−F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammograms and GCD curves of PPy-1, PPy-2 and PPy-3
ce:section.ce:para: To choose PPy with appropriate size for the electrostatic self-assembly with GQDs, the electrochemical properties of the three PPy samples are compared. Fig. 3 A displays the cyclic voltammograms of PPy-1/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and PPy-3/GCE at the scan rate of 5 mV s − 1. All the three samples exhibit a pair of weak redox peaks, indicating the pseudo-capacitive characteristic of PPy. Among the three PPy samples, PPy-2 displays the largest area under the cyclic voltammograms, suggesting the highest specific capacitance of PPy-2. Since PPy-1 is synthesized from the Py monomer of the lowest concentration, the polymer chain of PPy-1 is short and incomplete, leading to limited capacitive performance of PPy-1. Although PPy-3 is synthesized from the Py monomer of the highest concentration, the serious aggregation of the PPy-3 particles is inevitable, resulting in lower specific capacitance of PPy-3. As expected, the calculated specific capacitance of PPy-2 from Eq. (1) (278 F g − 1) is greatly higher than those of PPy-1 (183 F g − 1) and PPy-3 (212 F g − 1). The highest specific capacitance of PPy-2 can also be revealed by the GCD curves of PPy-1/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and PPy-3/GCE at the current density of 1 A g − 1. As shown in Fig. 3B, PPy-2 displays a longer discharging duration than PPy-1 and PPy-3, implying that PPy-2 possesses higher specific capacitance. According to Eq. (2), the specific capacitances of the three samples are calculated to be 157 (PPy-1), 258 (PPy-2) and 187 F g − 1 (PPy-3), respectively, agreeing well with Fig. 3B. Although PPy-3 is synthesized from Py of the highest concentration, the serious aggregation of the PPy-3 particles results in lower specific capacitance than PPy-2. Therefore, PPy-2 is chosen for the electrostatic self-assembly with GQDs.

To prepare ERGQDs/PPy-2, CV is used for the direct electrochemical reduction of GQDs/PPy-2, and the corresponding cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE in 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.0) are shown in Fig. 4 A. For comparison, the cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE without GQDs is also presented (Fig. 4B). There is a pronounced reduction peak at around −1.0 V on the first cycle on the cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE, which is attributed to the reduction of the oxygen-containing groups on the GQDs surface [35], since no obvious reduction peak is observed on the first cycle on the cyclic voltammograms of the bare GCE. Noted that the pronounced reduction peak weakens gradually in the next cycles until disappears completely on the fiftieth cycle owing to the complete depletion of the surface-oxygenated species (Fig. 4A). The depletion of the surface-oxygenated species at the surface of GQDs can be further confirmed by the differential pulse voltammograms of GQDs/PPy-2/GCE before and after its electrochemical reduction. As can be seen, an obvious reduction peak is observed at around −1.1 V before the electrochemical reduction GQDs/PPy-2 (Fig. 4C), which is caused by the reduction of the oxygen-containing groups. After the electrochemical reduction by CV for 50 cycles, the reduction peak disappears completely (Fig. 4D), suggesting that the reduction of GQDs is almost completed. That is to say, ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE is successfully prepared. Successful reduction of GQDs can also be confirmed by the deconvoluted C 1 s XPS spectra of GQDs/PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM images of ERGQDs and ERGQDs/PPy-2
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of ERGQDs and ERGQDs/PPy-2 are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the as-prepared ERGQDs are composed of nanoparticles with the uniform size of ~5 nm (Fig. 5A), and the ERGQDs can be well-dispersed within the matrix of PPy-2 (Fig. 5B). Fig. 5C shows the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of ERGQDs/PPy-2. An interlayer spacing of ~0.35 nm is observed, which is the typical characteristic of GQDs possessing the graphitic nature and high crystallinity [36]. The HRTEM image of ERGQDs/PPy-2 indicates that the electrochemical reduction does not alter the graphitic nature and high crystallinity of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns of different samples
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of different samples are shown in Fig. 6 A. As listed in Table 1 , the bands at 1766 and 1716 cm−1 are consistent with the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C = O group of the GQDs [37], and the band at 1182 cm−1 implies the existence of C − C − O group [38]. These characteristic bands of oxygen-containing groups disappear almost completely on the spectrum of ERGQDs, suggesting the complete depletion of the surface-oxygenated species via electrochemical reduction. In the case of PPy-2, the bands located at 1544, 1460, 1172 and 906 cm−1 can be assigned to the pyrrole ring (1544 cm−1), C − N stretching vibration (1460 cm−1), in-plain vibrations of C − H (1172 cm−1) and =C − H (906 cm−1), respectively [39]. Noted that these characteristic bands of PPy-2 can also be observed on the spectrum of ERGQDs/PPy-2, indicating the successful combination of ERGQDs and PPy-2.

The XRD patterns of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 are shown in Fig. 6B. For ERGQDs, the two diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 23.3° and 42.6° are indexed to the (002) and the (100) crystalline plane, respectively [40]. For PPy-2, the wide diffraction peak from 2θ = 15° to 35° can be ascribed to the oriented polymer chain of PPy [41]. In the case of ERGQDs/PPy-2, the diffraction peak centered at around 23° is broader than those of ERGQDs and PPy-2, which might be attributed to the superposition of the diffraction peaks of ERGQDs (23.3°) and PPy-2.

The cyclic voltammograms of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the scan rate of 5 mV s − 1 are shown in Fig. 7 A. The curves of ERGQDs display a quasi-rectangular shape, suggesting a typical characteristic of the ideal EDLC behavior of ERGQDs with high reversibility [42]. Both ERGQDs/PPy-2 and PPy-2 exhibit a pair of weak redox peaks, which can be ascribed to the pseudo-capacitive characteristic of PPy-2. Noted that the area under the curves of ERGQDs/PPy-2 is remarkably larger than those of PPy-2 and ERGQDs, implying the highest specific capacitance of ERGQDs/PPy-2. The cyclic voltammograms of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at different scan rates are presented in Fig. 7B. It shows that the shape of the curves remains almost unchanged with increasing scan rate from 5 to 100 mV s − 1, implying excellent rate capability of the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy-2. The specific capacitances of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 at various scan rates are calculated according to Eq. (1) and presented in Fig. 7C. As can be seen, the capacitances of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 at 5 mV s − 1 are 137, 278 and 438 F g − 1, respectively, while 126, 175 and 340 F g − 1 at 100 mV s − 1. When the scan rate increases to 100 mV s − 1, the capacitance retention of the three samples is calculated to be 92.0% (ERGQDs), 62.9% (PPy-2) and 77.6% (ERGQDs/PPy-2), respectively. Obviously, the good rate capability of ERGQDs/PPy-2 can be ascribed to the introduction of ERGQDs with typical EDLC characteristic to the hybrids. In addition, the stability of ERGQDs/PPy-2 is also tested by recording the cyclic voltammograms of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE in 1.0 M H2SO4 at the scan rate of 50 mV s − 1 for 50 consecutive cycles (Fig. S2 in Supporting Information), and the results clearly indicate the excellent stability of ERGQDs/PPy-2.

The GCD curves of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at the current density of 1 A g − 1 are shown in Fig. 8 A. The curves of ERGQDs present the shape of a symmetric triangle, further implying the EDLC characteristic of ERGQDs. Both PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 present a nonlinear shape, and this tailed phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of PPy-2 with pseudo-capacitive characteristic. Compared with ERGQDs and PPy-2, the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids exhibit a longer discharging duration, suggesting higher specific capacitance of the hybrids. Fig. 8B displays the GCD curves of ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE at different current densities. As expected, the specific capacitance of ERGQDs/PPy-2 decreases gradually with increasing current density from 1 to 10 A g − 1. The specific capacitances of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 at different current densities are calculated according to Eq. (2) and presented in Fig. 8C. It shows that the capacitances of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 at 1 A g − 1 are 123, 258 and 418 F g − 1, respectively, while 111, 160 and 317 F g − 1 at 10 A g − 1. When the current density increases from 1 to 10 A g − 1, the capacitance retention of the three samples is 90.2% (ERGQDs), 62.0% (PPy-2) and 75.8% (ERGQDs/PPy-2), respectively. This comparison further confirms that ERGQDs play an important role in the improvement of rate capability of the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids. In fact, the addition of graphene to hybrids for the enhancement of other performances has also been reported previously [43,44]. In addition, the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy-2 is also compared to other systems based on carbon-PPy [45–48] at the current density of 1 A g − 1, and the results are listed in Table 2 . As can be seen, the ERGQDs/PPy-2 exhibits higher specific capacitance at the same current density, which might be attributed to the fact that the nanosized zero-dimensional ERGQDs match well with the nanostructured PPy in size.

To get a deeper insight into the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids, the EIS of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE is studied. As shown in Fig. 9 , the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which is marked by the diameter of the semicircle at high frequency, of PPy-2 (curve c) is remarkably higher than those of ERGQDs (curve b) and ERGQDs/PPy-2 (curve a). This result implies that the introduction of ERGQDs with excellent electrical conductivity to PPy can greatly enhance the charge transfer at the electrode/solution interfaces, which is further confirmed by the measured electrical conductivity of different PPy samples (PPy-1, PPy-2, PPy-3) and ERGQDs/PPy-2. As shown in Table 3 , the electrical conductivity of PPy-2 (64 S m − 1) is higher than those of PPy-1 (27 S m − 1) and PPy-3 (13 S m − 1), agreeing well with the results of Fig. 3A. After the introduction of ERGQDs, the electrical conductivity of ERGQDs/PPy-2 increases significantly to 1253 S m − 1 owing to the excellent electrical conductivity of ERGQDs. From Fig. 9, it can also be observed that the Warburg resistance (Wd), which is marked by the slope of the inclined line at low frequency, of ERGQDs/PPy-2 is lower than those of ERGQDs and PPy-2. This result suggests that compared with PPy-2 and ERGQDs, the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids would make the diffusion of electrolyte ions easier.

Cyclic stability is an important factor which should be considered for supercapacitors, and thus the cyclic stability of ERGQDs/GCE, PPy-2/GCE and ERGQDs/PPy-2/GCE is studied and the results are shown in Fig. 10 A. After 1000 charging/discharging cycles, the capacitance retention of ERGQDs, PPy-2 and ERGQDs/PPy-2 is 98.8%, 68.1% and 86.0%, respectively, at the current density of 10 A g–1. The low capacitance retention of PPy-2 could be attributed to the volumetric change of PPy resulted from the repeated redox (charging/discharging) cycles [49]. Noted that the cyclic stability of ERGQDs and ERGQDs/PPy-2 is greatly higher than that of PPy-2, which might be due to the introduction of ERGQDs with robust mechanical strength. The cyclic stability of ERGQDs/PPy-2 is also studied under the condition of continuously altering the current density. As shown in Fig. 10B, the specific capacitance of ERGQDs/PPy-2 remains almost unchanged after the current density increases gradually from 1 to 10 A g − 1 and then suddenly turns back to 1 A g − 1, indicating excellent cyclic stability of the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids. The excellent stability of the ERGQDs/PPy-2 might be ascribed to the fact that the ERGQDs can provide an outstanding interface for the interactions between electrodes and electrolytes and significantly improve the electrochemical performance and cycling stability [50].

To summarize, the ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids were successfully prepared through the electrostatic self-assembly of GQDs and PPy, followed by the direct electrochemical reduction of GQDs. The particle size of PPy in the hybrids depends on the concentration of Py monomer, which affects the electrochemical performances of PPy. PPy-2 was chosen for the preparation of the hybrids owing to its highest specific capacitance. Due to the combination of the EDLC characteristic from ERGQDs and the pseudo-capacitive characteristic from PPy-2, the obtained ERGQDs/PPy-2 hybrids exhibit high specific capacitance and good rate capability. In addition, the introduction of ERGQDs with robust mechanical strength can also significantly enhance the cyclic stability of the hybrids. These findings indicate that the as-prepared ERGQDs/PPy-2 might be a potential and promising candidate as an advanced material to be used in energy conversion systems.

Xinmin Shi: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Zheng-Zhi Yin: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Jiale Xu: Investigation, Methodology. Shan Li: Investigation, Methodology. Chengchao Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Binghu Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Yong Qin: Methodology. Yong Kong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY18B050005), Natural Science Foundation for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (20KJA150005), Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis (SKLBA2108), Future Research Project of Jiangsu Province ( BE2017088 ) and Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center ( ACGM2016-06-27 ).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.138435.
